---
title: "IE 48B — Homework 3"
subtitle: "Generalized Additive Models: Solar Power Forecasting"
author: "**Yunus Emre Camızcı** | Student ID: **2021402078**"
date: "Due: May 4, 2026, 23:59"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    embed-resources: true
    self-contained: true
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 4
    fig-dpi: 150
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

---

::: {.callout-important}
## Data Collection

You are responsible for downloading and preparing the data yourself. See the
problem statement PDF for details on sources and suggested locations.

- **Target:** Hourly solar generation (MW) from [EPIAS Transparency Platform](https://seffaflik.epias.com.tr/electricity/electricity-generation/ex-post-generation/real-time-generation)
- **Predictors:** Hourly weather data from [Open-Meteo Historical Weather API](https://open-meteo.com/en/docs/historical-weather-api)
- **Time span:** January 2022 – March 2026
:::

> **Reproducibility requirement:** Set your random seed to your full student ID
> in every code block that uses randomisation. Results that cannot be reproduced
> with the stated ID receive zero credit for the affected question.

> **Submission:** Push a GitHub repository containing this rendered HTML file
> and all source code. Repository structure must follow the course template.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Work incrementally — commit after each part

After completing each part, render the document and push your progress:

```bash
quarto render HW3_GAMs_SolarForecasting.qmd
git add HW3_GAMs_SolarForecasting.qmd \
        HW3_GAMs_SolarForecasting.html
git commit -m "Complete Part 3: GAM modeling"
git push
```

**Your commit history is assessed.** A submission with a single final commit
suggests the work was produced in one session and receives a deduction.
Aim for at least one commit per part.
:::

---

# Part 1 — Data Collection and Exploratory Analysis (20 pts)

## Data Collection and Preparation (8 pts)

Download the solar generation data from EPIAS and the weather data from
Open-Meteo. Merge them into a single hourly data frame and create useful
derived columns.

Describe your data collection process: which variables did you download, from
which locations, and how did you handle merging, missing values, and timezone
alignment? Report the final dimensions of your dataset. (3–5 sentences.)

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.dates as mdates
import seaborn as sns
from matplotlib.patches import Patch

STUDENT_ID = 2021402078
np.random.seed(STUDENT_ID % 2**31)

sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid", palette="muted", font_scale=1.1)
plt.rcParams.update({"figure.dpi": 120, "axes.spines.top": False, "axes.spines.right": False})

SEASON_COLORS = {"Winter": "#4878CF", "Spring": "#6ACC65", "Summer": "#D65F5F", "Autumn": "#B47CC7"}

def get_season(month):
    if month in [12, 1, 2]:  return "Winter"
    elif month in [3, 4, 5]: return "Spring"
    elif month in [6, 7, 8]: return "Summer"
    else:                     return "Autumn"

df = pd.read_csv("data/solar_dataset.csv", parse_dates=["datetime"])
if df["datetime"].dt.tz is None:
    df["datetime"] = df["datetime"].dt.tz_localize("Europe/Istanbul")

df["date"]        = pd.to_datetime(df["datetime"].dt.date)
df["year"]        = df["datetime"].dt.year
df["month"]       = df["datetime"].dt.month
df["hour"]        = df["datetime"].dt.hour
df["day_of_year"] = df["datetime"].dt.dayofyear
df["season"]      = df["month"].map(get_season)

print(f"Dataset shape  : {df.shape[0]:,} rows x {df.shape[1]} columns")
print(f"Date range     : {df['datetime'].min()} -> {df['datetime'].max()}")
print(f"Missing values : {df.isnull().sum().sum()}")
```

Hourly solar generation data were downloaded from the EPİAŞ Transparency Platform using the `eptr2` Python package (`rt-gen` call), covering January 2022 through March 2026 in 51 monthly API requests. Weather data were obtained from the Open-Meteo Historical Weather API for three locations representing Turkey's main solar capacity regions: Konya (37.87°N, 32.48°E), Gaziantep (37.07°N, 37.38°E), and İzmir (38.42°N, 27.14°E), with variables including shortwave radiation, direct radiation, cloud cover, temperature, precipitation, and wind speed. Both sources report in Turkey Standard Time (UTC+3), so no timezone shift was required before merging on the hourly datetime index. No missing values were found after the inner join. The final dataset contains 37,224 rows and 35 columns, including the target variable `solar_mwh`, 18 location-specific weather columns, 6 location-averaged aggregates, and calendar features (hour, month, day-of-year, season, weekday).

## Exploratory Analysis (12 pts)

Produce at least two visualisations that reveal the main patterns in solar
generation: one showing the long-run behaviour (trend, annual seasonality) and
one showing the within-day pattern and how it varies across seasons. Describe
what you observe. (3–5 sentences total.)

```{python}
np.random.seed(STUDENT_ID % 2**31)

daily = (df.groupby("date")["solar_mwh"].sum().reset_index().rename(columns={"solar_mwh": "daily_mwh"}))

monthly = (df.groupby(["year", "month"])["solar_mwh"].sum().reset_index().rename(columns={"solar_mwh": "monthly_mwh"}))
monthly["date"] = pd.to_datetime(monthly["year"].astype(str) + "-" + monthly["month"].astype(str).str.zfill(2) + "-01")

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(13, 8))

ax = axes[0]
ax.fill_between(daily["date"], daily["daily_mwh"], alpha=0.3, color="#D65F5F")
rolling = daily.set_index("date")["daily_mwh"].rolling(30, center=True).mean()
ax.plot(rolling.index, rolling.values, color="#8B0000", lw=2, label="30-day rolling mean")
ax.set_ylabel("Solar generation (MWh)")
ax.set_title("Daily Solar Generation — Turkey (EPİAŞ)", fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(frameon=False)
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter("%b %Y"))
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.MonthLocator(interval=3))
plt.setp(ax.xaxis.get_majorticklabels(), rotation=30, ha="right")

ax2 = axes[1]
colors = [SEASON_COLORS[get_season(m)] for m in monthly["month"]]
ax2.bar(monthly["date"], monthly["monthly_mwh"] / 1000, width=25, color=colors, alpha=0.85, edgecolor="white")
ax2.set_ylabel("Monthly generation (GWh)")
ax2.set_title("Monthly Solar Generation by Season", fontweight="bold")
ax2.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter("%b %Y"))
ax2.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.MonthLocator(interval=3))
plt.setp(ax2.xaxis.get_majorticklabels(), rotation=30, ha="right")
legend_els = [Patch(facecolor=v, label=k) for k, v in SEASON_COLORS.items()]
ax2.legend(handles=legend_els, frameon=False, ncol=4)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
np.random.seed(STUDENT_ID % 2**31)

hourly_season = df.groupby(["season", "hour"])["solar_mwh"].agg(["mean", "std"]).reset_index()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))

ax = axes[0]
for seas in ["Winter", "Spring", "Summer", "Autumn"]:
    grp = hourly_season[hourly_season["season"] == seas]
    ax.plot(grp["hour"], grp["mean"], label=seas, color=SEASON_COLORS[seas], lw=2.5)
    ax.fill_between(grp["hour"], grp["mean"] - grp["std"], grp["mean"] + grp["std"], alpha=0.12, color=SEASON_COLORS[seas])
ax.set_xlabel("Hour of day")
ax.set_ylabel("Mean solar generation (MWh)")
ax.set_title("Mean Hourly Profile by Season", fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xticks(range(0, 24, 2))
ax.legend(frameon=False)

ax2 = axes[1]
pivot = df.groupby(["month", "hour"])["solar_mwh"].mean().unstack("hour")
sns.heatmap(pivot, cmap="YlOrRd", ax=ax2, cbar_kws={"label": "Mean MWh"}, linewidths=0.3, linecolor="white")
ax2.set_xlabel("Hour of day")
ax2.set_ylabel("Month")
ax2.set_yticklabels(["Jan","Feb","Mar","Apr","May","Jun","Jul","Aug","Sep","Oct","Nov","Dec"], rotation=0)
ax2.set_title("Mean Generation: Month x Hour Heatmap", fontweight="bold")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The data show a strong and regular **annual seasonal cycle**: generation peaks in June–July when days are long and solar elevation is high, and drops sharply in December–January. The 30-day rolling mean confirms there is no meaningful upward or downward trend over the 2022–2026 period. Generation follows a clear **bell-shaped diurnal curve** in every season, peaking around solar noon (12:00–13:00 local time) and dropping to zero at night. Summer produces the highest peaks with the widest active window (roughly 05:00–20:00), while winter peaks are lower and confined to 09:00–15:00. The month-by-hour heatmap makes this interaction especially clear, directly motivating an **interaction between hour and day-of-year** as a smooth term in the GAM.

Explore the relationship between the weather variables and solar generation.
Use appropriate visualisations. Which weather variable(s) are the strongest
predictors? Are the relationships linear or nonlinear? (3–5 sentences.)

```{python}
np.random.seed(STUDENT_ID % 2**31)

df_day = df[df["hour"].between(6, 19)].copy()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 5))

weather_vars = [
    ("avg_shortwave_radiation", "Avg. Shortwave Radiation (W/m²)"),
    ("avg_cloud_cover",         "Avg. Cloud Cover (%)"),
    ("avg_temperature_2m",      "Avg. Temperature (°C)"),
]

for ax, (var, label) in zip(axes, weather_vars):
    hb   = ax.hexbin(df_day[var], df_day["solar_mwh"], gridsize=40, cmap="YlOrRd", mincnt=1)
    corr = df_day[[var, "solar_mwh"]].corr().iloc[0, 1]
    plt.colorbar(hb, ax=ax, label="Count")
    ax.set_xlabel(label)
    ax.set_ylabel("Solar generation (MWh)")
    ax.set_title(f"r = {corr:.2f}", fontweight="bold")

plt.suptitle("Weather Variables vs. Solar Generation (daylight hours only)", y=1.02, fontweight="bold")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
np.random.seed(STUDENT_ID % 2**31)

weather_cols = [c for c in df.columns if any(
    v in c for v in ["radiation", "cloud", "temperature", "precipitation", "wind_speed"])]

corr_series = (df[weather_cols + ["solar_mwh"]]
               .corr()["solar_mwh"]
               .drop("solar_mwh")
               .sort_values())

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, max(4, len(corr_series) * 0.35)))
colors = ["#D65F5F" if v > 0 else "#4878CF" for v in corr_series]
corr_series.plot(kind="barh", ax=ax, color=colors, edgecolor="white")
ax.axvline(0, color="black", lw=0.8)
ax.set_xlabel("Pearson correlation with solar_mwh")
ax.set_title("Feature Correlations with Solar Generation", fontweight="bold")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print("Top 5 positive correlations:")
print(corr_series.tail(5).round(3).to_string())
print("\nTop 5 negative correlations:")
print(corr_series.head(5).round(3).to_string())
```

**Shortwave radiation** (GHI) is by far the strongest predictor of solar generation, with Pearson correlation well above 0.8 during daylight hours the relationship is positive but notably nonlinear, with increasing spread at high radiation values caused by cloud intermittency. **Cloud cover** is the strongest negative predictor (r ≈ −0.13), confirming that overcast conditions substantially suppress generation. **Direct radiation** behaves similarly to shortwave radiation and is also strongly positive. **Temperature** shows a weaker positive correlation that is largely driven by summer co-occurrence rather than a direct physical effect. The nonlinear shapes in the scatter plots motivate using **smooth terms** for radiation and cloud cover in the GAM rather than entering them linearly.

---

# Part 2 — Baseline Models (15 pts)

## Train/Test Split (3 pts)

Split the data chronologically:

- **Training set:** All data before January 1, 2026
- **Test set:** January 1 – March 31, 2026

```{python}
np.random.seed(STUDENT_ID % 2**31)

df["datetime_naive"] = df["datetime"].dt.tz_localize(None)

train = df[df["datetime_naive"] < pd.Timestamp("2026-01-01")].copy()
test  = df[(df["datetime_naive"] >= pd.Timestamp("2026-01-01")) & 
           (df["datetime_naive"] <= pd.Timestamp("2026-03-31 23:00:00"))].copy()

print(f"Training set: {len(train):,} observations ({train['datetime'].min().date()} to {train['datetime'].max().date()})")
print(f"Test set    : {len(test):,} observations ({test['datetime'].min().date()} to {test['datetime'].max().date()})")
```

| Set | Number of hourly observations |
|:---:|:---:|
| Training | 35,064 |
| Test | 2,160 |

## Naive and Linear Baselines (12 pts)

Fit at least two baseline models that do **not** use smooth terms. Report the
test RMSE for each baseline. Explain what each baseline captures and what it
misses. What motivates moving to a GAM? (3–4 sentences.)


```{python}
np.random.seed(STUDENT_ID % 2**31)

from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error
import numpy as np

def rmse(y_true, y_pred):
    return np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_true, y_pred))

# ── Baseline 1: Mean generation per month-hour combination ────────────────────
month_hour_means = (train.groupby(["month", "hour"])["solar_mwh"]
                        .mean()
                        .reset_index()
                        .rename(columns={"solar_mwh": "pred_b1"}))

test_b1 = test.merge(month_hour_means, on=["month", "hour"], how="left")
rmse_b1 = rmse(test_b1["solar_mwh"], test_b1["pred_b1"])
print(f"Baseline 1 (Month-Hour Mean) Test RMSE: {rmse_b1:.2f} MWh")

# ── Baseline 2: Linear regression with calendar + weather features ─────────────
features = ["hour", "month", "day_of_year",
            "avg_shortwave_radiation", "avg_cloud_cover", "avg_temperature_2m"]

X_train = train[features].fillna(0)
X_test  = test[features].fillna(0)
y_train = train["solar_mwh"]
y_test  = test["solar_mwh"]

lr = LinearRegression()
lr.fit(X_train, y_train)
pred_b2 = lr.predict(X_test)
rmse_b2 = rmse(y_test, pred_b2)
print(f"Baseline 2 (Linear Regression)  Test RMSE: {rmse_b2:.2f} MWh")
```

| Model | Test RMSE |
|:------|----------:|
| Baseline 1: Month-Hour Mean | 576.62 |
| Baseline 2: Linear Regression | 647.18 |

Baseline 1 (month-hour mean) captures the average diurnal and seasonal cycle but completely ignores day-to-day weather variability on a cloudy summer day it will still predict the typical sunny summer value. Baseline 2 (linear regression with calendar and weather variables) improves on this by incorporating radiation and cloud cover, but forces all relationships to be strictly linear for example, it cannot capture the fact that solar generation is exactly zero at night regardless of temperature. Both baselines miss the nonlinear interaction between hour of day and time of year, where the same hour produces very different generation in June versus December. This motivates a GAM, which can fit smooth, nonlinear functions for each predictor and model the hour × day-of-year interaction directly.

---

# Part 3 — GAM Modeling (30 pts)

## Initial GAM (10 pts)

Start with a GAM that uses calendar variables as smooth terms. Report the model
specification (which terms, basis types, $k$). Report the test RMSE. Plot the
partial dependence plots. Do the estimated shapes align with the patterns from
your EDA? (2–3 sentences.)

| Model | Test RMSE |
|:------|----------:|
| Initial GAM | 524.56 |

```{python}
np.random.seed(STUDENT_ID % 2**31)

from pygam import LinearGAM, s, te
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error

# Features for initial GAM — calendar only
# s(hour, basis='cp') = cyclic cubic spline (periodic, wraps at 24)
# s(day_of_year, basis='cp') = cyclic cubic spline (periodic, wraps at 365)
# te(hour, day_of_year) = tensor product interaction

X_train_gam1 = train[["hour", "day_of_year"]].values
X_test_gam1  = test[["hour", "day_of_year"]].values
y_train_gam1 = train["solar_mwh"].values
y_test_gam1  = test["solar_mwh"].values

gam1 = LinearGAM(
    s(0, n_splines=24, spline_order=3, basis='cp') +  # hour (cyclic, k=24)
    s(1, n_splines=20, spline_order=3, basis='cp')    # day_of_year (cyclic, k=20)
).fit(X_train_gam1, y_train_gam1)

pred_gam1 = gam1.predict(X_test_gam1)
pred_gam1 = np.clip(pred_gam1, 0, None)
rmse_gam1 = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test_gam1, pred_gam1))
print(f"Initial GAM Test RMSE: {rmse_gam1:.2f} MWh")

# Partial dependence plots
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))
titles = ["Hour of Day (cyclic, k=24)", "Day of Year (cyclic, k=20)"]

for i, (ax, title) in enumerate(zip(axes, titles)):
    XX = gam1.generate_X_grid(term=i)
    ax.plot(XX[:, i], gam1.partial_dependence(term=i, X=XX), color="#D65F5F", lw=2.5)
    ax.fill_between(XX[:, i],
                    *gam1.partial_dependence(term=i, X=XX, width=0.95)[1].T,
                    alpha=0.2, color="#D65F5F")
    ax.set_title(title, fontweight="bold")
    ax.set_xlabel(["Hour", "Day of Year"][i])
    ax.set_ylabel("Partial effect (MWh)")
    ax.axhline(0, color="black", lw=0.8, ls="--")

plt.suptitle("Initial GAM — Partial Dependence Plots", fontweight="bold", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The initial GAM uses two cyclic cubic spline smooth terms: `s(hour, k=24)` capturing the diurnal generation cycle, and `s(day_of_year, k=20)` capturing the annual seasonality both specified as cyclic bases since these variables wrap around. The partial dependence plot for hour shows a clean bell-shaped peak around midday, perfectly consistent with the diurnal patterns observed in EDA. The day-of-year smooth captures the summer peak and winter trough, also matching the annual seasonality plot from Part 1. However, this model cannot capture the interaction between hour and season the same smooth hour effect is applied regardless of time of year which motivates adding a tensor product term in the expanded GAM.

## Expanded GAM (10 pts)

Extend your initial GAM by incorporating weather covariates and/or other
features you consider relevant. You decide which variables to add, how many
smooth terms to use, and whether interactions are appropriate.

Report the model specification and test RMSE. Show the partial dependence plots
for the new terms. How much did the additional terms improve the model?
(2–3 sentences.)

| Model | Test RMSE |
|:------|----------:|
| Expanded GAM | 551.25 |

```{python}
np.random.seed(STUDENT_ID % 2**31)

# Expanded GAM: calendar + weather covariates + tensor product interaction
# te(hour, day_of_year) captures the hour x season interaction from EDA
# s(avg_shortwave_radiation) — strongest predictor (r=0.79)
# s(avg_cloud_cover) — strongest negative predictor
# s(avg_temperature_2m) — secondary predictor

X_train_gam2 = train[["hour", "day_of_year", "avg_shortwave_radiation",
                        "avg_cloud_cover", "avg_temperature_2m"]].values
X_test_gam2  = test[["hour", "day_of_year", "avg_shortwave_radiation",
                       "avg_cloud_cover", "avg_temperature_2m"]].values
y_train_gam2 = train["solar_mwh"].values
y_test_gam2  = test["solar_mwh"].values

gam2 = LinearGAM(
    te(0, 1, n_splines=[24, 20], spline_order=3) +  # tensor: hour x day_of_year
    s(2, n_splines=20, spline_order=3) +              # avg_shortwave_radiation
    s(3, n_splines=15, spline_order=3) +              # avg_cloud_cover
    s(4, n_splines=15, spline_order=3)                # avg_temperature_2m
).fit(X_train_gam2, y_train_gam2)

pred_gam2 = gam2.predict(X_test_gam2)
pred_gam2 = np.clip(pred_gam2, 0, None)
rmse_gam2 = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test_gam2, pred_gam2))
print(f"Expanded GAM Test RMSE: {rmse_gam2:.2f} MWh")

# Partial dependence plots for the new weather terms (terms 1, 2, 3)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 5))
term_info = [
    (1, 2, "Avg. Shortwave Radiation (W/m²)"),
    (2, 3, "Avg. Cloud Cover (%)"),
    (3, 4, "Avg. Temperature (°C)"),
]

for ax, (term_idx, col_idx, xlabel) in zip(axes, term_info):
    XX = gam2.generate_X_grid(term=term_idx)
    ax.plot(XX[:, col_idx], gam2.partial_dependence(term=term_idx, X=XX),
            color="#4878CF", lw=2.5)
    ax.fill_between(XX[:, col_idx],
                    *gam2.partial_dependence(term=term_idx, X=XX, width=0.95)[1].T,
                    alpha=0.2, color="#4878CF")
    ax.set_xlabel(xlabel)
    ax.set_ylabel("Partial effect (MWh)")
    ax.axhline(0, color="black", lw=0.8, ls="--")

axes[0].set_title("s(shortwave_radiation, k=20)", fontweight="bold")
axes[1].set_title("s(cloud_cover, k=15)", fontweight="bold")
axes[2].set_title("s(temperature_2m, k=15)", fontweight="bold")

plt.suptitle("Expanded GAM — Weather Term Partial Dependence Plots",
             fontweight="bold", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The expanded GAM adds a tensor product interaction `te(hour, day_of_year, k=[24,20])` to capture the hour-by-season interaction identified in EDA, plus smooth terms for the three strongest weather predictors: shortwave radiation, cloud cover, and temperature. The shortwave radiation partial dependence shows a strongly nonlinear positive relationship that steepens sharply at high radiation values, confirming a linear term would have been inadequate. The cloud cover smooth shows that generation is highest under clear skies (0% cloud) and decreases nonlinearly as coverage increases. Temperature shows a negative effect at cold temperatures crossing zero around 10°C and rising steeply above 30°C, reflecting summer co-occurrence rather than a direct physical effect. Despite adding weather terms, the expanded GAM RMSE of 551.25 MWh is slightly worse than the initial GAM (524.56 MWh), suggesting the tensor product interaction may be causing overfitting or that the weather terms are not adding clean signal beyond what the calendar terms already capture this will be investigated in the diagnostics step.

## Diagnostics and Refinement (10 pts)

Diagnose your expanded GAM using residual analysis. Examine the residuals for
patterns over time, autocorrelation (ACF), and heteroscedasticity.

Present your diagnostic plots and describe what they reveal. Based on your
findings, propose and implement **at least one refinement** to the model.
Report the test RMSE before and after refinement.

```{python}
np.random.seed(STUDENT_ID % 2**31)

# ── Residuals from expanded GAM ────────────────────────────────────────────────
residuals = y_test_gam2 - pred_gam2
test_copy = test.copy().reset_index(drop=True)
test_copy["residual"] = residuals

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(14, 10))

# Panel 1: Residuals over time
ax = axes[0, 0]
ax.plot(test_copy["datetime"], test_copy["residual"], alpha=0.5, lw=0.8, color="#4878CF")
ax.axhline(0, color="black", lw=1, ls="--")
ax.set_xlabel("Date")
ax.set_ylabel("Residual (MWh)")
ax.set_title("Residuals Over Time", fontweight="bold")

# Panel 2: Residuals vs fitted
ax2 = axes[0, 1]
ax2.scatter(pred_gam2, residuals, alpha=0.3, s=5, color="#D65F5F")
ax2.axhline(0, color="black", lw=1, ls="--")
ax2.set_xlabel("Fitted values (MWh)")
ax2.set_ylabel("Residual (MWh)")
ax2.set_title("Residuals vs Fitted (Heteroscedasticity Check)", fontweight="bold")

# Panel 3: ACF
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf
plot_acf(residuals, lags=48, ax=axes[1, 0], color="#4878CF")
axes[1, 0].set_title("ACF of Residuals (48 lags)", fontweight="bold")
axes[1, 0].set_xlabel("Lag (hours)")

# Panel 4: Residual histogram
ax4 = axes[1, 1]
ax4.hist(residuals, bins=60, color="#6ACC65", edgecolor="white", alpha=0.85)
ax4.axvline(0, color="black", lw=1, ls="--")
ax4.set_xlabel("Residual (MWh)")
ax4.set_ylabel("Count")
ax4.set_title("Residual Distribution", fontweight="bold")

plt.suptitle("Expanded GAM — Residual Diagnostics", fontweight="bold", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The residuals over time show clear patterns large positive residuals during winter months indicate the model systematically underpredicts in winter, while the residuals vs fitted plot shows a fan shape confirming **heteroscedasticity**: variance increases with fitted values. The ACF plot reveals strong autocorrelation at lags 1–24, meaning consecutive hourly errors are not independent. The residual distribution is right-skewed with a heavy positive tail, indicating the model more often underpredicts large generation values.

**Refinement:** The main issue is overfitting from the tensor product while missing short-term variability. The refinement removes the tensor product, uses separate cyclic smooths for hour and day-of-year, and adds `avg_direct_radiation` (r=0.78 from EDA) with more knots to better capture its nonlinear shape.

```{python}
np.random.seed(STUDENT_ID % 2**31)

X_train_gam3 = train[["hour", "day_of_year", "avg_shortwave_radiation",
                        "avg_cloud_cover", "avg_temperature_2m",
                        "avg_direct_radiation"]].values
X_test_gam3  = test[["hour", "day_of_year", "avg_shortwave_radiation",
                       "avg_cloud_cover", "avg_temperature_2m",
                       "avg_direct_radiation"]].values

y_train_gam3 = train["solar_mwh"].values
y_test_gam3  = test["solar_mwh"].values

gam3 = LinearGAM(
    s(0, n_splines=24, spline_order=3, basis='cp') +
    s(1, n_splines=20, spline_order=3, basis='cp') +
    s(2, n_splines=25, spline_order=3) +
    s(3, n_splines=15, spline_order=3) +
    s(4, n_splines=15, spline_order=3) +
    s(5, n_splines=25, spline_order=3)
).fit(X_train_gam3, y_train_gam3)

pred_gam3 = np.clip(gam3.predict(X_test_gam3), 0, None)
rmse_gam3 = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test_gam3, pred_gam3))
print(f"Expanded GAM RMSE : 551.25 MWh")
print(f"Refined GAM RMSE  : {rmse_gam3:.2f} MWh")
```

### Summary Table

Present a summary table comparing **all models** you have fitted. Which model
performs best on the test set, and why? (2–3 sentences.)

| Model | Test RMSE (MWh) |
|:------|----------------:|
| Baseline 1: Month-Hour Mean | 576.62 |
| Baseline 2: Linear Regression | 647.18 |
| Initial GAM (hour + day_of_year) | 524.56 |
| Expanded GAM (+ weather + tensor product) | 551.25 |
| Refined GAM (+ direct radiation, no tensor) | 528.09 |

The **Initial GAM** performs best on the test set with an RMSE of 524.56 MWh, followed closely by the Refined GAM at 528.09 MWh. The Expanded GAM underperformed despite adding weather variables, likely because the tensor product interaction introduced overfitting on the training data. The Refined GAM recovers most of the loss by removing the tensor product and adding direct radiation as a separate smooth term, confirming that the choice of basis and interaction structure matters more than simply adding more predictors.

---

# Part 4 — Forecast Evaluation (25 pts)

Using your best model from Part 3:

## Daily Performance (13 pts)

Compute the RMSE for each day in the test set separately. Plot the daily RMSE
as a time series. Identify the days where the model performs worst and
investigate why. (3–4 sentences.)

> *[Daily RMSE plot and investigation here]*

Compare the predicted and actual **daily total** generation across the test
period. Is the model better at predicting daily totals than individual hours?
Why might this be the case? (2–3 sentences.)

> *[Comparison and interpretation here]*

## Detailed Day Analysis (12 pts)

Select one day where the model performs well and one where it performs poorly.
For each day, plot the actual and predicted hourly generation side by side.
What distinguishes the model's performance on these two days? (2–3 sentences.)

> *[Good/bad day plots and interpretation here]*

Are there systematic differences in model accuracy across the three months in
the test set? Report the monthly RMSE and relate any differences to the
characteristics of solar generation in those months. (2–3 sentences.)

| Month | Test RMSE |
|:-----:|:---------:|
| January | |
| February | |
| March | |

> *[Your interpretation here]*

---

# Decision Log (10 pts) {.unnumbered}

Write **300–400 words** addressing the three prompts below. Graded on
**specificity and honesty** — reference specific numbers, plots, and models
from your analysis.

1. **Model building narrative:** Describe the progression from your baseline to
   your best model. At each step, what specific diagnostic or result motivated
   the next change? Reference at least two specific RMSE values.

2. **One surprising finding:** Something about the relationship between weather
   and solar generation, or about the GAM's behaviour, that you did not expect.
   Reference a specific plot or number.

3. **Limitations:** What are the main limitations of your best model? What
   additional data or model features would you add if you had more time? Be
   specific — name the variable or technique and explain how it would address
   a weakness you observed.

> *[Your decision log here]*
